ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Salads and similar mixed dishes) used as the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for Vietnamese lotus stem salad in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific Vietnamese dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Gỏi Ngó Sen Tôm Thịt is approximately 200 g, representing one plate as served in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Lotus Stem Salad with Shrimp and Pork (Gỏi Ngó Sen Tôm Thịt) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010573). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on lotus root / mixed vegetable salad with protein
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gỏi Ngó Sen Tôm Thịt. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for lotus root (GI ~45, low category) as the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. The dish is low in total carbohydrate (5.9 g/100g) and contains shrimp and pork protein which further attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (45 × 5.9 × 2) / 100 = 5.3 (low GL).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g — exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; fish sauce dressing is the primary contributor
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request fish sauce to be served separately or reduced, and limit portion to no more than 100 g to keep sodium intake below 810 mg per occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate at 5.9 g/100g and low GI (~45); shrimp and pork protein slow gastric emptying and stabilise postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; request reduced sugar in the dressing and monitor total caloric intake given the high fat content of 44.54 g/100g from peanuts and dressing oil.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat at 44.54 g/100g combined with high sodium at 810 mg/100g; saturated fat breakdown not available but pork and peanut oil contribute saturated and omega-6 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to high fat and sodium content; if consumed, request reduced fish sauce and peanut dressing, choose a smaller portion of approximately 100 g, and balance with low-sodium, low-fat foods throughout the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g worsens tissue swelling; pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifying inflammatory pathways; chili (nightshade) may trigger joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request chili-free preparation, substitute pork with additional shrimp to reduce arachidonic acid load, and request reduced fish sauce to lower sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gỏi Ngó Sen Tôm Thịt (Lotus Stem Salad with Shrimp and Pork) is a traditional Vietnamese cold salad featuring thinly sliced lotus stem (ngó sen) as the primary vegetable, combined with boiled shrimp and sliced pork, dressed with a tangy fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, and chili dressing, and garnished with crushed roasted peanuts, fried shallots, and fresh herbs such as mint and perilla. The dish is popular in southern Vietnam and is widely served in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore. The texture is crisp and refreshing from the lotus stem, with savoury-sweet-sour flavour notes from the dressing. Nutritionally, the dish is low in carbohydrate (5.9 g/100g) and has a low glycemic index (~45), making it suitable for blood glucose management; however, it is high in total fat (44.54 g/100g, largely from peanuts and dressing oil) and very high in sodium (810 mg/100g from fish sauce), which raises concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. It contains peanuts (allergen), crustaceans (shrimp), and fish (fish sauce). The dish is not suitable for hypertension patients without significant sodium reduction. It is typically served as a starter or side dish at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010573) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The dish is a well-documented Vietnamese dish served in Singapore. GI is an analogous estimate based on lotus root GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, which reduces GI confidence to medium for that sub-field, but overall record confidence remains high given exact HPB source match for all macros and micronutrients.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is accepted as confirmed zero per the HPB source for this preparation. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this Vietnamese lotus stem salad dish is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB source entry for this dish. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate based on lotus root GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. Halal status is not confirmed; fish sauce brands vary in halal certification. Peanut allergen is present — dish is not suitable for peanut-allergic individuals.
